draft.introduction: 2 Enoch (also called the Slavonic Apocalypse of Enoch or the Book of the Secrets of Enoch) survives only in Old Church Slavonic manuscripts, though it was almost certainly composed in Greek, possibly drawing on a Hebrew or Aramaic original. Its date is debated—proposals range from the first century BCE to the early medieval period—but the theological content of chapter 22 places it firmly within the tradition of Jewish angelomorphic transformation. Chapter 22 describes the most explicit deification of a human being in the Jewish pseudepigraphical tradition. Enoch stands before the face of God, and the Lord commands the archangel Michael to strip him of his earthly garments, anoint him with holy oil, and clothe him in garments of glory. Enoch looks at himself and discovers that he has become “like one of the glorious ones, and there was no observable difference.” He has been transformed from a human being into an angel—or, more precisely, into a being indistinguishable from the highest angels. This is not metaphor. The text presents it as an ontological change.

translation.text: And the Lord said to Michael: “Go and take Enoch from out of his earthly garments, and anoint him with my sweet ointment, and put him into the garments of my glory.” And Michael did just as the Lord told him. He anointed me and dressed me.
translation.original_text: И рече Господь Михаилу: Поими Еноха и совлеци его ризы земныя

translation.text: And the appearance of that ointment was greater than a great light, and its ointment was like sweet dew, and its fragrance like myrrh, shining like the sun’s ray. And I looked at myself, and I was like one of the glorious ones, and there was no observable difference.
translation.original_text: И обличие масти тоя паче света велика

translation.text: And the Lord summoned one of his archangels by name Vreveil, who was swifter in wisdom than the other archangels, who records all the Lord’s deeds. And the Lord said to Vreveil: “Bring out the books from my storehouses, and a reed of quick-writing, and give it to Enoch, and deliver to him the choice and comforting books out of my hand.”
translation.original_text: И призва Господь единого от архангел своих именем Вревеил

translation.text: And Vreveil hurried and brought me the books, marbled with myrrh. And he gave me the reed from his hand. And he told me all the works of heaven, earth, and sea, and all the elements, their passages and goings, and the thunderings of the thunders, the sun and moon, the goings and changings of the stars.
translation.original_text: И потщися Вревеил и принесе ми книги

translation.text: And I wrote down all the things, and all the courses of the seasons, and their times and their names. And I wrote three hundred and sixty-six books during thirty days and thirty nights. And Vreveil was telling me and I was writing, and I did not cease writing for thirty days and thirty nights.
translation.original_text: И написах вся вещи и вся течения времен

translator_notes.note: The stripping of earthly garments and clothing in garments of glory is a motif found across Jewish mystical literature, from the priestly vestments of the high priest (which symbolized the cosmos) to the heavenly garments in the Hekhalot literature. The anointing with “sweet ointment” parallels the anointing of the high priest in Exodus 30:22-33, but here it effects an ontological transformation rather than a consecration to office.

The declaration “I was like one of the glorious ones, and there was no observable difference” is the most unambiguous statement of human deification in the Jewish pseudepigraphical corpus. “The glorious ones” are the angels. Enoch has not merely been elevated or honoured; he has become indistinguishable from angelic beings. This tradition feeds directly into the later Metatron traditions in 3 Enoch, where Enoch is identified as the “lesser YHWH.”

Vreveil (also spelled Vrevoil or Pravuil) is the angelic scribe, a figure paralleling Thoth in Egyptian tradition and Nabu in Mesopotamian. The transmission of heavenly books to Enoch makes him not merely an angel but a possessor of divine knowledge—the same claim that Gnostic texts make for their initiates. The number 366 books may correspond to the days of the solar calendar, suggesting that Enoch’s knowledge encompasses all of time itself.
